content.text: A grace period is the window between announcing that a component is deprecated and actually removing support for it. Defining grace periods explicitly — rather than letting them drift based on whoever is pushing hardest — gives callers predictable time to migrate, gives the platform team a legitimate date to enforce removal, and creates a written record that makes audits and incident reviews straightforward.

attrs.alt: Grace period support tiers table: Full Support, Grace Period, Sunset, and Archived — showing what is available in each phase for bug fixes, security patches, new features, SLA, and API availability

content.text: Why Grace Periods Need Explicit Policy

Without a defined policy, grace periods become negotiated case-by-case. The result is a platform with a long tail of versions still in "deprecated" status months after the sunset date, because no team had the authority to enforce removal without risking a production incident. The negotiation also creates precedent: if Team A got a 90-day extension, Team B will ask for the same regardless of their actual migration complexity.
A grace period policy is not about being inflexible — it is about making the rules known in advance so that extension requests are the exception, not the default outcome.
For GenAI systems specifically, the grace period must account for two factors that do not appear in conventional API deprecation:
Eval validation takes time.

A team cannot simply swap a model ID and ship. They must run an eval suite against the successor, review the results, and potentially adjust prompts to compensate for behavioral differences between model versions. Budget 1–2 weeks of this work into the minimum grace period.
Output behavior changes are hard to detect automatically.

A migrated prompt that produces subtly different outputs may not trigger any error — it will silently degrade user experience until the next eval cycle or a user complaint surfaces the regression. Callers need enough grace period to run quality validation, not just swap config values.
Standard Grace Period Durations
The following durations apply by default. Each can be extended (see the extension policy below) but cannot be shortened unilaterally by the platform team after the deprecation notice has been issued.
Component type
Standard grace period
Rationale
Internal prompt template version
60 days
Eval validation + prompt adjustment + deployment cycle
Internal model config version
60 days
Same as prompt; model change often requires prompt adjustment
Provider model (external)
90 days minimum (or provider deadline, whichever is later)
Provider sets the ceiling; team needs 90 days to validate successor
Eval benchmark version
30 days
Lower risk; successor benchmark usually compatible
Prompt API schema version
90 days
Schema changes may affect multiple downstream services requiring coordination
RAG retrieval config (chunking, indexing)
45 days
Index rebuild time included in window

These are  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'calendar', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]}  days from the deprecation notice date, not business days. Calendar days are simpler to track and avoid disputes about holidays and sprint breaks.
Support Levels During the Grace Period
During the grace period, the deprecated component is still operational but receives reduced support. The exact support level depends on the component type:
What the Platform Team Provides
Critical bug fixes.

If the deprecated version has a bug that causes data loss, security exposure, or production outages, the platform team will patch it. Bug fixes that affect quality or performance (non-critical) are  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'not', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]}  backported — callers should migrate to the successor instead.
Security patches.
Any P1/P2 security issue in the deprecated component is patched regardless of its position in the grace period.
Migration assistance.
The platform team provides a migration guide, answers questions in a designated migration channel, and reviews migration PRs on request.
What the Platform Team Does Not Provide
New feature requests on the deprecated version — all new features go into the successor.
Performance improvements — callers who need better performance should migrate.
SLA guarantees beyond best-effort — the deprecated version may have added latency (see the enforcement mechanism below).
Dedicated oncall escalation for non-critical issues on the deprecated version.
Enforcement Mechanism During the Grace Period

Grace periods without enforcement create the same outcome as no grace period at all — indefinite support. The enforcement mechanism must be technical, not social, so it scales across many callers without manual follow-up:

The escalating latency penalty (0ms → 200ms → 500ms → hard error) means that callers who delay migration will see their SLOs degrade progressively. This creates a measurable reason to prioritize the migration work without causing outages.
Grace Period Extension Policy
Extensions are available but require a formal request and written approval. They are not automatic.
Eligibility for Extension
A caller may request an extension if they can demonstrate that migration within the standard window is not feasible due to:
External dependency on a third-party system that cannot be updated in the standard window.
A production incident that consumed the team's migration capacity during the grace period.
A quality regression in the successor component that the platform team has acknowledged but not yet resolved.
Regulatory review requirements that introduce a delay beyond the team's control.

A desire to prioritize feature work, insufficient staffing, or late discovery of the deprecation notice are  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'not', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  eligible reasons for extension.
Extension Request Process

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

ExtensionStatus = Literal["pending", "approved", "denied"]

@dataclass
class GracePeriodExtensionRequest:
    deprecated_asset_id: str
    caller_service: str
    current_sunset_date: date
    requested_extension_days: int   # must be ≤ 30
    reason: str
    blocking_dependency: str | None
    requestor_email: str
    status: ExtensionStatus = "pending"
    reviewer: str | None = None
    decision_date: date | None = None
    denial_reason: str | None = None

    @property
    def new_sunset_date(self) -> date:
        from datetime import timedelta
        return self.current_sunset_date + timedelta(days=self.requested_extension_days)

    def approve(self, reviewer: str) -> None:
        self.status = "approved"
        self.reviewer = reviewer
        self.decision_date = date.today()

    def deny(self, reviewer: str, reason: str) -> None:
        self.status = "denied"
        self.reviewer = reviewer
        self.decision_date = date.today()
        self.denial_reason = reason

def validate_extension_request(req: GracePeriodExtensionRequest) -> list[str]:
    """Return list of validation errors, empty if request is valid."""
    errors = []
    if req.requested_extension_days > 30:
        errors.append("Extension increment cannot exceed 30 days.")
    if not req.reason or len(req.reason) < 50:
        errors.append("Reason must be at least 50 characters — explain the specific blocker.")
    if req.current_sunset_date < date.today():
        errors.append("Cannot extend a grace period that has already expired — contact platform lead.")
    return errors
Extension Limits
Extension number
Max increment
Approval required from
Logged where
First extension
30 days
On-call platform engineer
Deprecation register
Second extension
30 days
Platform tech lead
Deprecation register + team lead email
Third+ extension
Not allowed without waiver
VP Engineering waiver
Escalation ticket required
Support Contract Considerations

If the GenAI platform serves external customers under a support contract, grace periods become contractual obligations. The internal policy must align with the terms of the most restrictive customer contract:
Enterprise contracts often guarantee 12-month deprecation notice.
If a provider model is deprecated with 90 days' notice, the platform team may need to maintain compatibility shims or host the model independently for the contracted window.
SLA language must reflect support tier changes.
During a grace period, if latency penalties are applied, the SLA should explicitly state that deprecated versions are excluded from latency guarantees.
Document the deprecation timeline in release notes.
External customers need written evidence of when deprecation was announced and when the grace period began. Maintain a public changelog that records every deprecation event with dates.
Tracking the Deprecation Register

Every deprecation event — internal and provider-driven — should be recorded in a single deprecation register. The register is the authoritative source of truth for what is deprecated, what is sunsetted, and who has approved extensions:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

DeprecationStatus = Literal["active", "deprecated", "sunset", "archived"]

@dataclass
class DeprecationEntry:
    asset_id: str                       # e.g. "prompt:support-reply:v3"
    asset_type: str                     # "prompt", "model", "eval", "schema"
    status: DeprecationStatus
    deprecated_date: date | None
    sunset_date: date | None
    successor_asset_id: str | None
    impacted_services: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    extensions: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)   # list of extension records
    notes: str = ""

    def total_grace_days(self) -> int | None:
        if not self.deprecated_date or not self.sunset_date:
            return None
        return (self.sunset_date - self.deprecated_date).days

class DeprecationRegister:
    def __init__(self):
        self._entries: dict[str, DeprecationEntry] = {}

    def register(self, entry: DeprecationEntry) -> None:
        if entry.asset_id in self._entries:
            raise ValueError(f"Asset {entry.asset_id!r} already registered.")
        self._entries[entry.asset_id] = entry

    def get_overdue(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> list[DeprecationEntry]:
        """Return entries whose sunset_date has passed but status is still 'deprecated'."""
        as_of = as_of or date.today()
        return [
            e for e in self._entries.values()
            if e.status == "deprecated"
            and e.sunset_date is not None
            and e.sunset_date < as_of
        ]

    def get_expiring_soon(self, days: int = 14) -> list[DeprecationEntry]:
        """Return entries whose grace period ends within the next N days."""
        from datetime import timedelta
        cutoff = date.today() + timedelta(days=days)
        return [
            e for e in self._entries.values()
            if e.status == "deprecated"
            and e.sunset_date is not None
            and e.sunset_date <= cutoff
        ]

The register should be queried nightly. Any entry that is overdue (sunset_date passed but status still "deprecated") should trigger an alert to the platform team — the sunset was not enforced and needs immediate attention.
Summary
Grace periods work when the rules are known in advance and enforced technically rather than socially. The key decisions to make and publish:
Standard grace period durations by component type — these are the defaults every caller can depend on.
Support levels during the grace period — what the platform team will and will not fix.
An escalating enforcement mechanism (latency penalty → hard error) that makes migration the path of least resistance.
A documented extension request process with explicit eligibility criteria and approval chain.
A deprecation register that serves as the authoritative record of all deprecated components and their status.
With these elements in place, grace periods become a tool for managing orderly migrations rather than a negotiation that produces inconsistent outcomes.
